narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 38 and an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, along with middle family income and plans to attend full-time, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 45.4% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to help ensure we admit students most likely to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 decile of 1, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure, well below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed in that direction, reinforcing concerns about consistency in your grades compared to peers who ranked higher. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the most influential positive factor, pulling strongly toward a prediction of passing since it exceeds the average of about 36.7 and shows real promise in analytical skills. Your undergraduate GPA, though below the typical 3.21 average, still nudged things slightly toward passing, highlighting some strengths in your prior college work. Less impactful were your full-time enrollment plans, which very mildly supported a passing prediction as that's the norm for 93% of students, and your middle family income, which had minimal sway either way. Overall, while your LSAT gives you an edge above many applicants, the low decile rankings in Years 1 and 3 were the biggest hurdles, placing you below typical students who pass the bar. I know this rejection stings, especially with your background, but it's not the end of the road—consider retaking courses or building a stronger academic record to boost those decile rankings, perhaps through post-bac programs or targeted prep. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or appeal with updated achievements. Keep pushing; many successful lawyers faced early setbacks like this. We're rooting for your next steps.
